topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white of the lab seemed to mock me. I stared at the schematics spread across the table, the lines of code, the meticulously drawn blueprints—they were mine, yet they felt like echoes of someone else's obsessive labor. When I finally pieced together the final component, the realization hit me like a physical blow: I had been building this exact system, line by agonizing line, for months. Across the room, hunched over a nearly identical workstation, was another figure. A slow, cold burn started low in my gut, tightening my jaw. I picked up a heavy wrench, the metal cool against my palm. My breath hitched, shallow and sharp. I didn't speak; I just moved, a deliberate, measured stride toward the other desk, every muscle coiled, ready to dismantle whatever fragile edifice they had constructed.

[story 2]

It was the silence that grated on my nerves most. The air in the archive room was thick, heavy with the scent of aged paper and ozone. I found the ledger, the handwritten notes detailing the iterative design process, and the matching project file. My hands began to tremble, not from fatigue, but from a rising tide of intense displeasure. Who else possessed this exact sequence of failures and triumphs? I watched the silhouette at the far end of the aisle, their posture mirroring my own coiled tension. A slow, deliberate intake of breath. I smoothed the creases from the paper, a tiny, almost imperceptible movement that held immense weight. Then, I stood up. The sound of my boots on the polished floor was unnaturally loud. I started walking, not toward the exit, but toward the source of the duplication, my gaze fixed, unwavering.

[story 3]

I traced the identical wiring diagram with a fingertip, the paper feeling flimsy under the pressure of my mounting frustration. It was absurd. Every calculation, every late night fueled by caffeine and sheer stubbornness, had been replicated. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I looked up, and there they were—the other person, equally engrossed, equally immersed in the very same obsessive task. A low growl escaped my throat, rough and involuntary. I clenched my fists until my knuckles were white shards of bone. I didn't want to shout; the anger demanded precision. I took a step forward, my eyes locking onto theirs, a silent declaration that this symmetry was about to be violently disrupted.
